issues.original: If your team trains or serves any model on Alibaba Cloud, you'll eventually use the PAI console. PAI is the umbrella; underneath it are the actual workhorses — a notebook product, a distributed training service, a model-serving service, and a few GUI/quick-deploy layers. After about eighteen months of running real LLM workloads on it for an AI marketing platform, this series is the field guide I wish I had before shipping my first endpoint.
issues.fixed: If your team trains or serves models on Alibaba Cloud, you'll eventually use the PAI console. PAI is the umbrella; underneath it are the actual workhorses — a notebook product, a distributed training service, a model-serving service, and a few GUI/quick-deploy layers. After about eighteen months of running real LLM workloads on it for an AI marketing platform, this series is the field guide I wish I had before deploying my first endpoint.

issues.original: This first article is the lay of the land. It is deliberately short on code — articles 2 to 5 are the deep dives. The goal is to help you understand what 'DLC job' and 'EAS endpoint' mean.
issues.fixed: This first article provides an overview. It is deliberately light on code — articles 2 to 5 dive deeper. The goal is to help you understand what 'DLC job' and 'EAS endpoint' mean.

issues.original: Per the official docs, **Platform for AI (PAI)** is 'Alibaba Cloud's AI development platform covering the full lifecycle: data annotation, model development, training, and deployment'. The console at [CODE] is one entry point, but PAI itself is a *family* of related products that share an account model, an OSS-backed storage layer, and a single Python SDK.
issues.fixed: According to the official docs, **Platform for AI (PAI)** is 'Alibaba Cloud's AI development platform covering the full lifecycle: data annotation, model development, training, and deployment.' The console at [CODE] is one entry point, but PAI itself is a *family* of related products that share an account model, an OSS-backed storage layer, and a single Python SDK.

issues.original: The mental model that has worked best for me: - **PAI is the shop.** - **DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, Model Gallery** are the workbenches inside. - **ECS, OSS, NAS, CPFS** are where the actual silicon and bytes live. PAI just orchestrates them on your behalf.
issues.fixed: The mental model that works best for me: - **PAI is the shop.** - **DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, Model Gallery** are the workbenches inside. - **ECS, OSS, NAS, CPFS** are where the actual silicon and bytes live. PAI orchestrates them on your behalf.

issues.original: Read it from the bottom up. The infrastructure layer includes CPUs, GPUs, RDMA fabric, and ACK Kubernetes. Above that, *Lingjun* (灵骏) provides high-density AI compute, while *general-purpose compute* offers everyday ECS-backed GPU pools. The platform-and-tools layer is where you spend most of your time: PyTorch, Megatron, DeepSpeed, PAI's optimization tools (TorchAcc, BladeLLM, EasyCkpt, AIMaster), and the visible products (DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, FeatureStore, iTAG). The application layer integrates PAI with Alibaba's MaaS world (ModelScope, Bailian/DashScope, Model Studio). The business layer covers industry use cases.
issues.fixed: Read it from the bottom up. The infrastructure layer includes CPUs, GPUs, RDMA fabric, and ACK Kubernetes. Above that, *Lingjun* (灵骏) provides high-density AI compute, while *general-purpose compute* offers everyday ECS-backed GPU pools. The platform-and-tools layer is where you spend most of your time: PyTorch, Megatron, DeepSpeed, PAI's optimization tools (TorchAcc, BladeLLM, EasyCkpt, AIMaster), and the visible products (DSW, DLC, EAS, Designer, FeatureStore, iTAG). The application layer integrates PAI with Alibaba's MaaS world (ModelScope, Bailian/DashScope, Model Studio). The business layer covers industry use cases.

issues.original: Use PAI instead of raw ECS because it pre-configures CUDA and PyTorch images, mounts your OSS bucket, provides a metrics dashboard, and bills per second.
issues.fixed: Use PAI instead of raw ECS because it pre-configures CUDA and PyTorch images, mounts your OSS bucket, provides a metrics dashboard, and bills per second.

issues.original: After a year and a half of production work I have only ever paid for these, drawn straight from the official 'Core components' table:
issues.fixed: After a year and a half of production work, I have only ever paid for these, drawn straight from the official 'Core components' table:

issues.original: There's also **iTAG** (data annotation), **PAI-Lingjun** for large clusters, **PAI-Blade / BladeLLM** for inference optimization, and **FeatureStore**. Unless you're doing >1000-GPU pretraining or building a recommender system, you can ignore them initially.
issues.fixed: There's also **iTAG** (data annotation), **PAI-Lingjun** for large clusters, **PAI-Blade / BladeLLM** for inference optimization, and **FeatureStore**. Unless you're doing >1000-GPU pretraining or building a recommender system, you can initially ignore them.

issues.original: Designer and Model Gallery are orthogonal — they sit on top, generating jobs that ultimately run on the same DLC / EAS substrate.
issues.fixed: Designer and Model Gallery are orthogonal — they sit on top, generating jobs that run on the same DLC / EAS substrate.

issues.original: This trips up everyone who comes from a pure cloud-VM background. Three rules: 1. **PAI never owns your data.** Datasets, checkpoints, and model artifacts all live in **OSS** (or **NAS** for POSIX semantics, or **CPFS** for HPC-style throughput). When a DSW or DLC instance dies, anything you didn't write to OSS is gone. There is a 'system disk' but treat it as [CODE].
issues.fixed: This trips up everyone who comes from a pure cloud-VM background. Three rules: 1. **PAI never owns your data.** Datasets, checkpoints, and model artifacts all live in **OSS** (or **NAS** for POSIX semantics, or **CPFS** for HPC-style throughput). When a DSW or DLC instance dies, anything not written to OSS is gone. There is a 'system disk,' but treat it as [CODE].

issues.original: 2. **PAI does own the compute.** You do not provision GPU ECS instances yourself for PAI workloads. PAI manages a pool, you ask for [CODE] and you get billed per second of allocation.
issues.fixed: 2. **PAI does own the compute.** You don't provision GPU ECS instances yourself for PAI workloads. PAI manages a pool, you request [CODE], and you get billed per second of allocation.

issues.original: 3. **PAI shares your account but uses its own RAM roles.** When you grant PAI access to OSS, you're attaching a service-linked role ([CODE]) so PAI's compute can read your bucket without a long-lived AK pair. Do not skip this — without it your DLC jobs will fail at [CODE] time with a 403.
issues.fixed: 3. **PAI shares your account but uses its own RAM roles.** When you grant PAI access to OSS, you attach a service-linked role ([CODE]) so PAI's compute can read your bucket without a long-lived AK pair. Do not skip this — without it, your DLC jobs will fail at [CODE] time with a 403.

issues.original: > **Real-world tip:** The single most common 'PAI is broken' ticket is a permission issue between PAI and OSS. Before debugging your training script, run [CODE] from inside a DSW terminal. If that fails, fix the role, not the code.
issues.fixed: > **Real-world tip:** The most common 'PAI is broken' ticket is a permission issue between PAI and OSS. Before debugging your training script, run [CODE] from inside a DSW terminal. If that fails, fix the role, not the code.

issues.original: To get started you need three things in this order: 1. **An aliyun.com account** with real-name verification (实名认证) — required for any GPU resource. International accounts work for most regions but Hangzhou, Shanghai, and Beijing have the best GPU stock.
issues.fixed: To get started, you need three things in this order: 1. **An aliyun.com account** with real-name verification (实名认证) — required for any GPU resource. International accounts work for most regions, but Hangzhou, Shanghai, and Beijing have the best GPU stock.

issues.original: 2. **A region.** Pick one and stick to it. PAI resources, OSS buckets, and ECS GPUs are all region-scoped, and cross-region traffic costs money and adds latency. For mainland production I default to [CODE]; for international, [CODE] (Singapore).
issues.fixed: 2. **A region.** Pick one and stick to it. PAI resources, OSS buckets, and ECS GPUs are all region-scoped, and cross-region traffic costs money and adds latency. For mainland production, I default to [CODE]; for international, [CODE] (Singapore).

issues.original: 3. **A workspace.** Per the docs, the workspace is PAI's tenancy primitive — it holds quotas, datasets, model registries, and IAM bindings. You almost always want at least two: a [CODE] workspace where humans poke around in DSW, and a [CODE] workspace where DLC jobs and EAS endpoints live. Cross-workspace permissioning is fiddly, but the isolation pays for itself the first time an intern accidentally restarts a serving endpoint.
issues.fixed: 3. **A workspace.** According to the docs, the workspace is PAI's tenancy primitive — it holds quotas, datasets, model registries, and IAM bindings. You almost always want at least two: a [CODE] workspace for human interaction in DSW, and a [CODE] workspace for DLC jobs and EAS endpoints. Cross-workspace permissioning is fiddly, but the isolation pays for itself the first time an intern accidentally restarts a serving endpoint.

issues.original: Like Bailian, PAI gives you two ways to do everything. The console is good for one-offs and inspecting state; the SDK is what you ship in CI
issues.fixed: Like Bailian, PAI gives you two ways to do everything. The console is good for one-offs and inspecting state; the SDK is what you use in CI.

issues.original: Every time I onboard a new ML engineer to PAI the first day looks the same. They start a DSW instance, [CODE] their world, train for an hour, restart the kernel for some reason, and then ask me where their model file went. The honest answer — "in [CODE] on a node that no longer exists" — is the kind of lesson you only need to learn once. This article is the version of that lesson you read in advance.
issues.fixed: Every time I onboard a new ML engineer to PAI, the first day is always the same. They start a DSW instance, [CODE] their world, train for an hour, restart the kernel for some reason, and then ask me where their model file went. The honest answer — "in [CODE] on a node that no longer exists" — is a lesson you only need to learn once. This article is the version of that lesson you can read in advance.

issues.original: Per the official "DSW Overview", DSW is a **cloud-based IDE for AI development** that integrates JupyterLab, VSCode, and a terminal, with pre-configured container images for PyTorch and TensorFlow, heterogeneous compute (CPU / GPU / Lingjun), and the ability to mount datasets from OSS, NAS, and CPFS. In practice that means you click "Open" and within a minute you have a real Jupyter on a real GPU with [CODE] working and PyTorch already importable.
issues.fixed: According to the official "DSW Overview," DSW is a **cloud-based IDE for AI development** that integrates JupyterLab, VSCode, and a terminal, with pre-configured container images for PyTorch and TensorFlow, heterogeneous compute (CPU / GPU / Lingjun), and the ability to mount datasets from OSS, NAS, and CPFS. In practice, this means you click "Open" and within a minute, you have a real Jupyter on a real GPU with [CODE] working and PyTorch already importable.

issues.original: The DSW container has a system disk that lives only as long as the instance does. Anything you [CODE] survives a kernel restart but does not survive an instance restart unless you persist the conda env to OSS or save it to ACR via the snapshot feature.
issues.fixed: The DSW container's system disk exists only as long as the instance does. Anything you [CODE] survives a kernel restart but not an instance restart unless you persist the conda environment to OSS or save it to ACR via the snapshot feature.

issues.original: According to the docs, DSW resource types come in two flavors: **public resources** (pay-as-you-go) and **dedicated resources** (subscription on general-purpose compute or Lingjun). For day-to-day work, public resources are the right choice — you pay for the GPU minutes you actually use, and the per-second metering lets you spin one up for a 10-minute experiment without worry.
issues.fixed: According to the docs, DSW resource types come in two flavors: **public resources** (pay-as-you-go) and **dedicated resources** (subscription on general-purpose compute or Lingjun). For day-to-day work, public resources are the best choice — you pay for the GPU minutes you use, and the per-second metering lets you spin one up for a 10-minute experiment without worry.

issues.original: What I actually pick: - **Tiny experiment / debugging** — [CODE] (1 × A10, 24 GB). Cheap, plenty for fine-tuning a 7B in 4-bit quant or running diffusion at 512×512. - **Real training of a small model** — [CODE] or [CODE] (A10 / A100 40 GB). Comfortable for a CIFAR-10 ResNet, ImageNet-tiny, or a 7B SFT with QLoRA. - **LLM dev** — [CODE] or higher (A100 80 GB). Required if you want to load a 13-30B in BF16 without offloading.
issues.fixed: What I actually pick: - **Tiny experiment / debugging** — [CODE] (1 × A10, 24 GB). Cheap and sufficient for fine-tuning a 7B in 4-bit quant or running diffusion at 512×512. - **Real training of a small model** — [CODE] or [CODE] (A10 / A100 40 GB). Suitable for a CIFAR-10 ResNet, ImageNet-tiny, or a 7B SFT with QLoRA. - **LLM dev** — [CODE] or higher (A100 80 GB). Required if you want to load a 13-30B in BF16 without offloading.

issues.original: The console flow looks like this: The lifecycle hooks are easy to ignore and expensive to forget. **Idle shutdown** at 30 minutes is my default; **scheduled shutdown** at 11pm catches the case where I leave a notebook running over the weekend. Every DSW idle GPU at 5 RMB an hour is roughly 100 RMB you owe Aliyun by Monday.
issues.fixed: The console flow looks like this: The lifecycle hooks are easy to ignore and costly to forget. **Idle shutdown** at 30 minutes is my default; **scheduled shutdown** at 11pm catches the case where I leave a notebook running over the weekend. Each idle DSW GPU costs 5 RMB an hour, which adds up to about 100 RMB owed to Aliyun by Monday.

issues.original: The single most important diagram in the entire DSW docs: The mount path I use everywhere: Mounting OSS is configured at instance create time; the docs call it "Configure storage." Pick the bucket and the prefix, choose mount path [CODE], accept the default access mode (FUSE-backed). After launch, [CODE] should work from the terminal — that is your "PAI ↔ OSS RAM role" health check.
issues.fixed: The single most important diagram in the DSW docs: The mount path I use everywhere: Mounting OSS is configured when creating the instance; the docs call it "Configure storage." Pick the bucket and the prefix, choose the mount path [CODE], and accept the default access mode (FUSE-backed). After launch, [CODE] should work from the terminal — that's your "PAI ↔ OSS RAM role" health check.

issues.original: The Quick Start expects roughly 98% validation accuracy after 3 epochs on a single A10. If you see anything dramatically lower, you've probably mounted OSS wrong and are reading from the wrong directory — not a model bug.
issues.fixed: The Quick Start expects about 98% validation accuracy after 3 epochs on a single A10. If you see significantly lower results, you've likely mounted OSS incorrectly and are reading from the wrong directory — not a model bug.

issues.original: DSW ships TensorBoard as a built-in extension; the docs walk through enabling it from the menu. I usually just run it as a cell: The link the docs tell you to click is [CODE] — DSW proxies the port so it works in your browser through the DSW URL. If the port is "in use," another notebook in the same instance is holding it; restart the kernel of the offender, not the instance.
issues.fixed: DSW includes TensorBoard as a built-in extension; the docs explain how to enable it from the menu. I usually just run it as a cell: The link the docs provide is [CODE] — DSW proxies the port so it works in your browser through the DSW URL. If the port is "in use," another notebook in the same instance is using it; restart the kernel of the offending notebook, not the entire instance.

issues.original: DSW has two mechanisms here, both worth knowing: 1. **Instance image snapshot** — bakes your current container state (installed packages, system files) to ACR. Next instance you start, pick that image and you are back where you left off. Slow (a few minutes) but exact. 2. **Conda env on OSS** — install all your [CODE] deps under [CODE] and activate it. Survives instance death without rebaking. Faster but does not capture system-level changes ([CODE] etc).
issues.fixed: DSW has two mechanisms, both worth knowing: 1. **Instance image snapshot** — saves your current container state (installed packages, system files) to ACR. When you start the next instance, pick that image, and you're back where you left off. It's slow (a few minutes) but exact. 2. **Conda env on OSS** — install all your [CODE] dependencies under [CODE] and activate it. This survives instance death without rebaking. It's faster but doesn't capture system-level changes ([CODE] etc).

issues.original: A DSW notebook is for one engineer on one GPU. When you need eight GPUs across two nodes, or when training runs longer than eight hours, you switch to **DLC**. DLC is PAI's job-submission front-end for a managed Kubernetes cluster: you describe what you want (image, command, resources, data mounts), DLC schedules pods, runs them to completion, persists logs, and tells you what happened. The docs call this *Deep Learning Containers*; we just say "DLC job".
issues.fixed: A DSW notebook is for one engineer on one GPU. When you need eight GPUs across two nodes or training that runs longer than eight hours, you switch to **DLC**. DLC is PAI's job-submission front-end for a managed Kubernetes cluster. You describe what you want (image, command, resources, data mounts), and DLC schedules pods, runs them to completion, persists logs, and reports the results. The docs call this *Deep Learning Containers*; we just say "DLC job".

issues.original: The official DLC overview lists four bullets I want to highlight, because they matter:
issues.fixed: The official DLC overview highlights four key points that matter:

issues.original: The first and third points are what make DLC interesting compared to renting GPU ECS yourself.
issues.fixed: The first and third points are what make DLC more interesting than renting GPU ECS yourself.

issues.original: Two of those phases — **scheduler places pods** and **mount OSS / NAS** — are where almost all of the "my job is stuck in PENDING" tickets get filed. Stuck on schedule means your resource group is full; stuck on mount means your storage RAM role is wrong. Same as DSW, the diagnostic move is to spin up a tiny DSW with the same OSS mount and confirm [CODE] works.
issues.fixed: Two of these phases — **scheduler places pods** and **mount OSS / NAS** — are where most "my job is stuck in PENDING" tickets arise. If it's stuck on scheduling, your resource group is full. If it's stuck on mounting, your storage RAM role is incorrect. Just like with DSW, the diagnostic step is to spin up a small DSW with the same OSS mount and confirm [CODE] works.

issues.original: For most teams, the best choice is general-purpose, pay-as-you-go. Lingjun is useful when you're training with more than 8 GPUs and need RDMA between nodes. The docs mention that RDMA is configurable on Lingjun and provides "accelerated inter-node communication" (meaning NCCL AllReduce will be 5-10x faster than over standard Ethernet). Preemptible is a cost-effective option for jobs that checkpoint cleanly, which, thanks to EasyCKPT, is true for most jobs.
issues.fixed: For most teams, the best choice is the general-purpose, pay-as-you-go pool. Lingjun is useful when you're training with more than 8 GPUs and need RDMA between nodes. The docs mention that RDMA is configurable on Lingjun and provides "accelerated inter-node communication" (meaning NCCL AllReduce will be 5-10x faster than over standard Ethernet). Preemptible is a cost-effective option for jobs that checkpoint cleanly, which, thanks to EasyCKPT, is true for most jobs.

issues.original: A few things worth noting that are not obvious from a quick read of the docs:
issues.fixed: A few important points that aren't immediately clear from the docs:

issues.fixed: The console's "Training Jobs" view provides logs, GPU utilization, network throughput, and AIMaster events. You can also stream logs from the SDK:

issues.original: For longer jobs I forward logs to SLS (Log Service) and set CloudMonitor alerts on [CODE] for 15 minutes — that is the canonical signal that something is wedged on data loading or distributed init.
issues.fixed: For longer jobs, I forward logs to SLS (Log Service) and set CloudMonitor alerts on [CODE] for 15 minutes. This is the canonical signal that something is stuck on data loading or distributed init.

issues.original: AIMaster runs as a sidecar pod alongside your training pods, with its own ServiceAccount that has the necessary K8s permissions to mark, evict, and recreate worker pods. It performs three tasks:
issues.fixed: AIMaster runs as a sidecar pod alongside your training pods, with its own ServiceAccount that has the necessary K8s permissions to mark, evict, and recreate worker pods. It performs three tasks:

issues.original: EAS is where the money goes. DSW costs a few hundred RMB a month for development. DLC costs spike. EAS bills 24/7 because someone might call your endpoint, and the 'minimum replica count' in the autoscaler config is the most critical setting in the entire platform. This article covers what I wish I'd known before shipping our first production endpoint.
issues.fixed: EAS is where the money goes. DSW costs a few hundred RMB a month for development. DLC costs spike. EAS bills 24/7 because someone might call your endpoint, and the 'minimum replica count' in the autoscaler config is the most critical setting in the entire platform. This article covers what I wish I'd known before deploying our first production endpoint.

issues.original: The official 'EAS overview' describes it as: 'deploy trained models as online inference services or AI web applications, with heterogeneous resources, automatic scaling, one-click stress testing, canary releases, and real-time monitoring'. The two key points are:
issues.fixed: The official 'EAS overview' describes it as: 'deploy trained models as online inference services or AI web applications, with heterogeneous resources, automatic scaling, one-click stress testing, canary releases, and real-time monitoring.' The two key points are:

issues.original: It's a **container-runtime serving layer** — your model is in OSS, your code is in a container image, EAS pulls the image, mounts OSS at startup, runs your start command, and listens on a port.
issues.fixed: It's a **container-runtime serving layer** — your model is in OSS, your code is in a container image, EAS pulls the image, mounts OSS at startup, runs your start command, and listens on a port.

issues.original: It's **autoscaled by replica count** — not a serverless function model (with one important exception, see below). Replicas are real GPU pods that take 30-120 seconds to start. Plan accordingly.
issues.fixed: It's **autoscaled by replica count** — not a serverless function model (with one important exception, see below). Replicas are real GPU pods that take 30-120 seconds to start. Plan accordingly.

issues.original: The four components the docs highlight for runtime-image deployment:
issues.fixed: The four components the docs highlight for runtime-image deployment:

issues.original: **Runtime image** — read-only template with OS, CUDA, Python, deps. Use an official one ([CODE], [CODE]) or push your own to ACR.
issues.fixed: **Runtime image** — read-only template with OS, CUDA, Python, and dependencies. Use an official one ([CODE], [CODE]) or push your own to ACR.

issues.original: **Code and model** — *not in the image*. They live in OSS / NAS. Decoupling them allows you to update weights without rebuilding the image.
issues.fixed: **Code and model** — *not in the image*. They live in OSS/NAS. Decoupling them allows you to update weights without rebuilding the image.

issues.original: **Storage mounting** — at startup, EAS FUSE-mounts the OSS path you specified to a directory inside the container, e.g. [CODE].
issues.fixed: **Storage mounting** — at startup, EAS FUSE-mounts the OSS path you specified to a directory inside the container, e.g., [CODE].

issues.original: **Run command** — the first command after the container starts. Typically launches your HTTP server ([CODE]).
issues.fixed: **Run command** — the first command after the container starts. Typically, it launches your HTTP server ([CODE]).

issues.original: > **Real-world tip:** Bake [CODE] into your code paths from day one. Do not let model paths get hardcoded to [CODE]. Switching from local-dev to EAS becomes a one-line config change instead of a code refactor.
issues.fixed: > **Real-world tip:** Bake [CODE] into your code paths from day one. Do not hardcode model paths to [CODE]. Switching from local-dev to EAS becomes a one-line config change instead of a code refactor.

issues.original: The docs list three. Pick deliberately — the wrong mode wastes either money or latency.
issues.fixed: The docs list three. Choose carefully — the wrong mode wastes either money or latency.

issues.original: A practical guideline:
issues.fixed: A practical guideline:

issues.original: - **Real-time sync** — chatbots, RAG retrieval, ad ranking, search. You care about p99 latency.
issues.fixed: - **Real-time sync** — chatbots, RAG retrieval, ad ranking, search. You care about p99 latency.

issues.original: - **Async** — anything that takes 5+ seconds: image-gen, video-gen, OCR-on-PDF batches. The built-in queue scales replicas based on backlog, which is the right approach for these workloads.
issues.fixed: - **Async** — anything that takes 5+ seconds: image-gen, video-gen, OCR-on-PDF batches. The built-in queue scales replicas based on backlog, which is the right approach for these workloads.

issues.original: - **Batch** — anything you can wait minutes for: nightly embeddings, voice transcription. Use preemptible instances and halve the cost.
issues.fixed: - **Batch** — anything you can wait minutes for: nightly embeddings, voice transcription. Use preemptible instances and halve the cost.

issues.fixed: The official Quick Start deploys Qwen3-0.6B with vLLM. The console process is:

issues.fixed: You then get an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at the URL the console provides. Call it:

issues.original: If that returns a sentence, your endpoint is live, and you can return to your colleagues looking like a wizard.
issues.fixed: If that returns a sentence, your endpoint is live, and you can return to your colleagues looking like a wizard.

issues.original: This is the part the docs don't emphasize. Default autoscaler behavior (scaling on request rate, with a minimum of 1 replica) can lead to cold-start latency issues or unexpected bills.
issues.fixed: This is the part the docs don't emphasize. Default autoscaler behavior (scaling on request rate with a minimum of 1 replica) can lead to cold-start latency issues or unexpected bills.

issues.original: The three settings that actually matter:
issues.fixed: The three settings that matter:

issues.original: - **[CODE]** — never set to zero in production. A cold start on a 7B vLLM container is 60-120 seconds; the user gives up at 5. I default to 2 (one for HA, one for redundancy). For asynchronous services you can do 0 and rely on the queue.
issues.fixed: - **[CODE]** — never set to zero in production. A cold start on a 7B vLLM container takes 60-120 seconds; users typically give up after 5. I default to 2 (one for HA, one for redundancy). For asynchronous services, you can set it to 0 and rely on the queue.

issues.original: - **[CODE]** — the budget brake. Calculate as: [CODE]. If you don't know your per-replica QPS, run the one-click stress test. The docs cover this under 'Service stress testing'.
issues.fixed: - **[CODE]** — the budget brake. Calculate as: [CODE]. If you don't know your per-replica QPS, run the one-click stress test. The docs cover this under 'Service stress testing'.

issues.original: - **Scaling metric** — by default it's [CODE]. For LLM serving, switch to [CODE] (or vLLM's [CODE] metric). QPS is misleading because long generations don't register as additional requests.
issues.fixed: - **Scaling metric** — by default, it's [CODE]. For LLM serving, switch to [CODE] (or vLLM's [CODE] metric). QPS is misleading because long generations don't register as additional requests.

issues.original: > **Real-world tip:** The single biggest wasted spend I have ever seen on PAI was a [CODE] autoscaler with [CODE] on a service that got 0.5 QPS off-peak. 5 idle A10s, 24/7, for two months. Always look at the Saturday-night dashboard before you go on holiday.
issues.fixed: > **Real-world tip:** The single biggest wasted spend I've ever seen on PAI was a [CODE] autoscaler with [CODE] on a service that got 0.5 QPS off-peak. 5 idle A10s, 24/7, for two months. Always check the Saturday-night dashboard before you go on holiday.

issues.original: EAS does this with **service groups**: a routing front-end that points at multiple service versions and splits traffic by percentage. The same primitive supports **traffic mirroring** — a copy of real traffic gets sent to a candidate version, but the response is discarded so users see no impact. This is the safest possible way to test a new model on production traffic.
issues.fixed: EAS does this with **service groups**: a routing front-end that points to multiple service versions and splits traffic by percentage. The same primitive supports **traffic mirroring** — a copy of real traffic is sent to a candidate version, but the response is discarded so users see no impact. This is the safest way to test a new model on production traffic.

issues.original: I use a 90/10 split for the first 24 hours of any model swap, then 50/50, and finally 0/100. If any step shows degradation in success rate or p99 metrics, rollback is immediate — service groups change traffic weights in seconds.
issues.fixed: I use a 90/10 split for the first 24 hours of any model swap, then 50/50, and finally 0/100. If any step shows degradation in success rate or p99 metrics, rollback is immediate — service groups change traffic weights in seconds.

issues.original: The docs have a whole section on the one-click stress tester. Use it. It auto-ramps QPS, charts replica scale-out, and tells you the per-replica saturation point. That number is what you build your autoscaler around. Deploying without one is the most common cause of 'the model fell over at the 3pm peak' tickets.
issues.fixed: The docs have a whole section on the one-click stress tester. Use it. It auto-ramps QPS, charts replica scale-out, and tells you the per-replica saturation point. That number is what you build your autoscaler around. Deploying without it is the most common cause of 'the model fell over at the 3pm peak' tickets.

issues.original: Buried in the docs: 'If an EAS service remains in a non-Running state for 180 consecutive days, the system automatically deletes the service.' Set a calendar reminder. I lost a service config once because the team that owned it dissolved and no one paid the bill. Restoring took an afternoon of re-bisecting which [CODE] version was on which weights.
issues.fixed: Buried in the docs: 'If an EAS service remains in a non-Running state for 180 consecutive days, the system automatically deletes the service.' Set a calendar reminder. I once lost a service config because the team that owned it dissolved and no one paid the bill. Restoring it took an afternoon of re-bisecting which [CODE] version was on which weights.

issues.original: Cold start is EAS's biggest practical issue. A vLLM Qwen3-7B container takes 60-120 seconds from scheduler pick to first token served — model load alone takes 30-60 seconds. If your autoscaler needs to add a replica under load, the first user requests in that window will time out.
issues.fixed: Cold start is EAS's biggest practical issue. A vLLM Qwen3-7B container takes 60-120 seconds from scheduler pick to first token served — model load alone takes 30-60 seconds. If your autoscaler needs to add a replica under load, the first user requests in that window will time out.

issues.original: The first four articles covered the underlying primitives — DSW, DLC, EAS — that you orchestrate with Python. This one focuses on two GUI products that wrap these primitives and provide a runnable solution for users who don't want to write Python: **PAI-Designer** for drag-and-drop tabular pipelines, and **Model Gallery** for zero-code open-source model deployment and fine-tuning. While serious engineers might not reach for them first, they are the right choice in two specific situations.
issues.fixed: The first four articles covered the underlying primitives — DSW, DLC, EAS — that you orchestrate with Python. This one focuses on two GUI products that wrap these primitives and provide a runnable solution for users who don't want to write Python: **PAI-Designer** for drag-and-drop tabular pipelines, and **Model Gallery** for zero-code open-source model deployment and fine-tuning. While serious engineers might not use them first, they are the right choice in two specific situations.

issues.original: According to the docs, Designer implements modeling and model debugging through workflows. Users can build AI development processes by dragging and dropping different components, like building blocks. Key features include 140+ built-in algorithm components, JSON export, scheduling in DataWorks, and support for custom SQL, Python, and PyAlink scripts as nodes.
issues.fixed: According to the docs, Designer implements modeling and model debugging through workflows. Users can build AI development processes by dragging and dropping different components, like building blocks. Key features include over 140 built-in algorithm components, JSON export, scheduling in DataWorks, and support for custom SQL, Python, and PyAlink scripts as nodes.

issues.original: Where it shines: - **Tabular ML at MaxCompute scale.** Designer is tightly bound to MaxCompute. If your training data is a 200-million-row partitioned table on MaxCompute, Designer's built-in source / split / encode / train components run inside MaxCompute itself, not over the wire to a Python pod. You are paying for MaxCompute compute, not GPU pods sitting idle waiting on data.
issues.fixed: Where it shines: - **Tabular ML at MaxCompute scale.** Designer is tightly bound to MaxCompute. If your training data is a 200-million-row partitioned table on MaxCompute, Designer's built-in source, split, encode, and train components run inside MaxCompute itself, not over the wire to a Python pod. You pay for MaxCompute compute, not idle GPU pods waiting on data.

issues.original: For fine-tuning, the docs provide a logistics-information-extraction example: feed it a JSON dataset, select LoRA hyperparameters from a dropdown, and it submits a DLC job for you. The Quick Start highlights the **distillation pattern** — using a large teacher (Qwen3-235B) to label data and a small student (Qwen3-0.6B) to learn from it. This pattern is worth internalizing; it's the most cost-effective fine-tuning method I know.
issues.fixed: For fine-tuning, the docs provide a logistics-information-extraction example: feed it a JSON dataset, select LoRA hyperparameters from a dropdown, and it submits a DLC job for you. This pattern is worth internalizing; it's the most cost-effective fine-tuning method I know.

issues.original: Summary heuristic: **start as high up the stack as the requirements allow**. Many teams over-engineer on day one, building custom DLC + EAS pipelines when a Model Gallery deploy would suffice. Optimize for time-to-first-token, then refactor once you have real traffic and metrics to design against.
